en.extract:
The thick-billed cuckoo (Pachycoccyx audeberti) is a species of cuckoo in the family Cuculidae. It is monotypic within the genus Pachycoccyx. It can easily be distinguished from other brood parasitic cuckoo species by its very thick bill, which is shaped in a rather hawk-like fashion.


Taxonomy
In a molecular genetic study by Sorenson and Payne (2005), the thick-billed cuckoo appeared to be most closely related to the koels, consisting of the dwarf koel (Microdynamis parva) and the true koels (Eudynamys), although the evidence was not very strong and further research was required.


Range and habitat
The thick-billed cuckoo is found in humid to subhumid woodlands and gallery forests from Guinea in the northwest to Mozambique in the southeast, though it is absent from large areas of the Congo Basin. It formerly occurred in the rainforests of northeastern Madagascar, but has not been reported from that island since 1922. In its southernmost regions the thick-billed cuckoo is partially migratory, spending the southern dry season in East Africa.


Habits
It is a typical cuckoo in terms of behaviour, feeding on hairy caterpillars, but also having a strong preference for grasshoppers and praying mantises.


Breeding
The thick-billed cuckoo is a specialised brood parasite of helmetshrikes, being known to almost exclusive parasitise three species in the red-billed and chestnut-fronted helmetshrike in its eastern range and the chestnut-bellied helmetshrike in West Africa, so limiting its breeding range to closed canopy forest habitat of the host in this region. In the case of the well-studied red-billed helmetshrike, the thick-billed cuckoo has caused probably the most dramatic reduction in nesting success by any brood parasitic bird, with one group of helmetshrikes unable to rear any of their own nestlings for five years and ten breeding attempts, with overall between 35 and 55 percent of nests parasitised. It is almost certain that the extinct Malagasy subspecies parasitised vangas, which are related to the helmetshrikes. This species' parasitism of the helmetshrikes may complement the similarly sized black cuckoo which exclusively parasitises bush shrikes.


References


External links
Image at ADW
Thick-billed cuckoo - Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds.

The '''thick-billed cuckoo''' ('''''Pachycoccyx audeberti''''') is a species of [[cuckoo]] in the family [[Cuculidae]]. It is [[monotypic]] within the genus '''''Pachycoccyx'''''.<ref>{{cite book |author=Mary Katherine Rowan |year=1983 |title=The Doves, Parrots, Louries, and Cuckoos of Southern Africa |publisher=[[Taylor & Francis]] |isbn=978-0-908396-66-5 |chapter=Genus ''Pachycoccyx'' Cabvanis, 1882 |pages=290–297 |chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qrsOAAAAQAAJ&pg=PA290}}</ref> It can easily be distinguished from other brood parasitic cuckoo species by its very thick bill, which is shaped in a rather hawk-like fashion.

==Taxonomy==
In a molecular genetic study by Sorenson and Payne (2005), the thick-billed cuckoo appeared to be most closely related to the koels, consisting of the [[dwarf koel]] (''Microdynamis parva'') and the true [[koel]]s (''Eudynamys''), although the evidence was not very strong and further research was required.<ref>Sorenson, Michael D.; [[Robert B. Payne|Payne, Robert B.]] (2005). "A molecular genetic analysis of cuckoo phylogeny". In Payne, Robert B. ''The Cuckoos''. Oxford University Press. pp. 90, 93. {{ISBN|0-19-850213-3}}.</ref>

==Range and habitat==
The thick-billed cuckoo is found in humid to subhumid woodlands and [[gallery forests]] from [[Guinea]] in the northwest to [[Mozambique]] in the southeast, though it is absent from large areas of the [[Congo Basin]].<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /><ref name="avian">[[Paul Johnsgard|Johnsgard, Paul A.]]; ''The Avian Brood Parasites: Deception at the Nest''; pp. 159-161 {{ISBN|0195110420}}</ref> It formerly occurred in the rainforests of northeastern [[Madagascar]], but has not been reported from that island since 1922.<ref name="guide">Langrand, Olivier; ''Guide to the Birds of Madagascar''; pp. 212-213. {{ISBN|0300043104}}</ref> In its southernmost regions the thick-billed cuckoo is partially migratory, spending the southern [[dry season]] in East Africa.<ref>Newman, Kenneth; ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=yYqLZtf5w0gC&q=%22Thick-billed+cuckoo%22 Newman's Birds of Southern Africa]''; p. 232. {{ISBN|1868727351}}</ref>

==Habits==
<!--[[image:Pachycoccyx audeberti, Gorongosa, Sofala, Birding Weto, a.jpg|thumb|left|Adult bird in miombo woodland, Mozambique]]-->
It is a typical cuckoo in terms of behaviour, feeding on hairy [[caterpillar]]s, but also having a strong preference for [[grasshopper]]s and [[praying mantis]]es.<ref>MacLean, Gordon; Roberts' Birds of Southern Africa (Sixth Edition); p. 330 {{ISBN|9780620175838}}</ref>

==Breeding==
The thick-billed cuckoo is a specialised [[brood parasite]] of [[helmetshrikes]],<ref name="avian"/> being known to almost exclusive parasitise three species in the [[red-billed helmetshrike|red-billed]] and [[chestnut-fronted helmetshrike]] in its eastern range and the [[chestnut-bellied helmetshrike]] in West Africa, so limiting its breeding range to closed canopy forest habitat of the host in this region.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Allport|first1=Gary|last2=Fanshawe|first2=John|date=1994|title=Is the Thick-billed Cuckoo Pachycoccyx audeberti a forest dependent species in West Africa?|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/302960010|journal=Malimbus|volume=16|pages=52–53}}</ref> In the case of the well-studied red-billed helmetshrike, the thick-billed cuckoo has caused probably the most dramatic reduction in nesting success by any brood parasitic bird, with one group of helmetshrikes unable to rear any of their own nestlings for five years and ten breeding attempts,<ref name="avian"/> with overall between 35 and 55 percent of nests parasitised. It is almost certain that the extinct Malagasy subspecies parasitised [[vanga]]s,<ref name="guide"/> which are related to the helmetshrikes. This species' parasitism of the helmetshrikes may complement the similarly sized [[black cuckoo]] which exclusively parasitises [[bush shrike]]s.
